stories.title: Multi-repo environments for cloud agents
stories.summary: Cursor's cloud agents now support multi-repo environments, letting you configure a single persistent environment containing multiple repositories that an agent can access across sessions. Under the hood, the environment is provisioned once and reused — agents don't re-clone or re-bootstrap on each run, which eliminates cold-start overhead for large dependency graphs. Multiple agents can run in parallel against the same environment configuration, enabling fleet-style execution across repos. This means you can now run an agent that, say, updates a shared protobuf schema in one repo and propagates the generated client changes into three downstream service repos in a single coordinated task — something that previously required manual orchestration or separate agent runs.
stories.considerations: The most concrete win is for cross-cutting refactors: imagine renaming a core API type that spans a monorepo split across auth-service, api-gateway, and frontend — a single agent can now traverse all three, make consistent changes, and validate them together rather than requiring a human to stitch together three separate sessions. The non-obvious implication is that environment reuse introduces shared state risk: if one agent run leaves a repo in a dirty or partially-modified state, subsequent sessions inherit that mess, so teams need disciplined cleanup hooks or snapshot/restore semantics baked into their workflows. This lands at exactly the moment teams are hitting the ceiling of single-repo agents — the most common complaint being that real tasks don't respect repo boundaries, and workarounds like copying files between contexts were lossy and error-prone. The honest gotcha: 'fully control' is doing a lot of work in the announcement — teams will need to think carefully about secrets management, network egress, and whether their CI environment assumptions hold when agents are mutating multiple repos concurrently rather than sequentially.

stories.title: Claude Opus 4.7 fast mode now available
stories.summary: Windsurf has added a 'fast mode' for Claude Opus 4.7 that delivers approximately 2.5x higher output token throughput compared to the standard Opus 4.7 mode. The speed increase likely comes from infrastructure-level optimizations — speculative decoding, batching strategies, or dedicated capacity routing — rather than model quantization or capability reduction, since Windsurf explicitly claims full model parity. Previously, developers using Opus 4.7 in Windsurf had to choose between the slower standard mode or dropping down to a less capable model for speed-sensitive tasks; fast mode removes that trade-off. This means long-file refactors, multi-step code generation, and analysis tasks that previously felt sluggish at Opus-class intelligence can now run at speeds closer to Sonnet-tier latency.
stories.considerations: In a concrete scenario: generating a 400-line TypeScript service with full docstrings and tests — a task that might take 45-60 seconds at standard Opus 4.7 speeds — could now complete in roughly 18-24 seconds, keeping developers in flow rather than context-switching while waiting. The non-obvious implication is that 2.5x throughput on a verbose model like Opus 4.7 may actually make agentic multi-step tasks (where latency compounds across tool calls) dramatically more practical than the raw multiplier suggests — a 5-step Cascade flow could go from 4 minutes to under 2. This matters now because Sonnet 3.7 and similar mid-tier models have been the de facto choice for interactive coding precisely because Opus-class models were too slow for iterative use; fast mode directly attacks that adoption bottleneck. The honest gotcha: 'fast mode' availability may be credit-tier or plan-gated in Windsurf, and if it consumes credits at a higher rate than standard mode, the effective cost-per-token could offset the speed benefit for heavy users — worth checking the billing docs before defaulting to it.

stories.title: Amazon Q Developer agentic capabilities on SWE-Bench
stories.summary: Amazon Q Developer's agentic capabilities claimed top positions on both SWE-Bench and SWE-Bench Lite leaderboards, which evaluate automated software engineering on real GitHub issues from open-source Python repos. SWE-Bench measures an agent's ability to resolve issues end-to-end: reading context, writing patches, and passing existing test suites — no human in the loop. The benchmark covers multistep tasks including unit test generation, documentation updates, and code review automation. This positions Amazon Q Developer as the current top-ranked agent for autonomous issue resolution on standardized, reproducible tasks — though the specific resolution rate and the date of the benchmark submission are not disclosed in this announcement.
stories.considerations: The SWE-Bench leaderboard is a legitimate signal — it uses real GitHub issues and requires patches to pass existing test suites, so it's harder to game than internal benchmarks — but 'highest score' without citing the actual pass rate (e.g., 'resolved X% of 300 tasks') makes it impossible to assess the absolute capability gap versus competitors like Devin or SWE-agent. In practice, a developer using Q Developer's agent on a mid-sized Python service could plausibly automate first-pass bug fixes and test scaffolding, but SWE-Bench is Python-only and open-source repos — performance on internal Java or TypeScript monorepos with proprietary frameworks is an open question. The timing matters: SWE-Bench scores have been climbing fast across all vendors in 2024-2025, so leaderboard positions shift monthly and 'highest score today' can be 'third place next sprint.' The honest gotcha here is that benchmark-leading performance on isolated issue resolution doesn't directly translate to multi-repo, multi-service agentic workflows where context windows, tool call limits, and organizational access controls dominate the failure modes — those aren't tested by SWE-Bench at all.

stories.title: Filter session history by workspace directory
stories.summary: Continue.dev added workspace-directory filtering to session history, letting users scope their chat log view to a specific folder rather than seeing all sessions globally. The filter works by associating each session with the workspace root active at session creation time, then surfacing only matching sessions when a filter is applied. No new API parameters or config changes are required — it's a UI-level filter on existing session metadata. Developers juggling multiple repos can now open a project and immediately see only the chat history relevant to that codebase, without manually scanning through unrelated sessions.
stories.considerations: If you're context-switching between, say, a frontend monorepo and a separate backend service daily, this directly cuts the time spent hunting for 'that session where I debugged the auth middleware' — previously you'd scroll through sessions from all projects mixed together. The non-obvious implication: session metadata must have been recording workspace path all along (or is being backfilled), which means older sessions may not filter correctly and could silently disappear from filtered views even when relevant. This lands at a moment when AI-assisted coding sessions are multiplying fast — developers are accumulating dozens of sessions per week, and global history is becoming genuinely unmanageable. Worth noting: if you work with multiple folders open in a single VS Code workspace (multi-root workspaces), it's unclear which root gets associated with a session, so filtering behavior there may be inconsistent and worth testing before relying on it.

stories.title: Claude Code Hooks: Synchronous Middleware for Every Tool Call
stories.summary: Claude Code now exposes a synchronous hook system that intercepts every tool call at named lifecycle checkpoints — PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, Stop, and roughly 20 others — running inside the agentic loop before results are returned to the model. Each hook receives a structured JSON payload on stdin and returns a decision object that can block, approve, modify, or annotate the event; a PreToolUse hook exiting with code 2 plus a JSON body cancels the tool call entirely and injects an explanation directly into Claude's context. Hooks are implemented as shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts — no SDK changes or internal patching required. This means developers can now enforce write policies, mutate prompts, inject secrets, or gate destructive operations using ordinary shell scripts wired to a config file.
stories.considerations: When iterating over a 300-file monorepo with Claude Code doing automated refactors, a PreToolUse hook on the Write tool can validate every file write against a schema or lint rule before it commits — catching drift that would otherwise require a separate review pass or a post-hoc diff. The non-obvious implication: because hooks run synchronously inside the loop and can inject context back to the model, you can build stateful guardrails — e.g., a hook that tracks cumulative file deletions and blocks further deletes once a threshold is hit, without Claude having any awareness of the counter. This lands at exactly the right moment: as Claude Code gets used in CI pipelines and unattended agents, the absence of interception points was the primary reason teams were wrapping it in fragile shell scaffolding or avoiding it for write-heavy tasks entirely. The honest gotcha: hooks are shell-level, so latency is real — every tool call now pays the fork/exec cost of your hook process, and a slow HTTP endpoint hook on PostToolUse will stall the entire loop; there's no async escape hatch, so you need to keep hook implementations lean or accept measurable slowdown on high-frequency tool calls.
